description: Iraq's invasion of Kuwait removed a major OPEC producer from the market and raised fears of wider Middle East supply disruption. Brent jumped from roughly $17 to over $40/bbl within weeks.

event_name: Gulf War / Operation Desert Storm Begins
description: The US-led coalition's military response to the Kuwait invasion quickly restored confidence that supply would be protected. Prices fell sharply from their pre-war spike, crashing back below $20/bbl within weeks.
The collapse of the Thai baht triggered a broader Asian financial crisis that sharply reduced regional oil demand, contributing to a multi-year price slide into 1998-99.
OPEC and non-OPEC producers agreed to significant coordinated production cuts, helping prices recover from the post-Asian-crisis lows of under $10/bbl in late 1998.
The attacks triggered a sharp but short-lived price shock amid fears of broader instability and a subsequent global economic slowdown that weighed on oil demand.
The onset of the Iraq War raised fears of extended Middle East supply disruption, contributing to sustained upward pressure on oil prices through the mid-2000s.
Brent crude hit an all-time nominal high amid strong emerging-market demand (especially China), a weak US dollar, and limited spare capacity, before collapsing months later.
The collapse of Lehman Brothers intensified the global financial crisis, collapsing demand expectations. Brent fell from ~$147 to under $40/bbl within six months, a decline of more than 70%.

event_name: Arab Spring / Libyan Civil War Onset
description: Widespread unrest across the Middle East and North Africa, including the outbreak of civil war in Libya, disrupted regional production and pushed Brent above $100/bbl.

With US shale output surging and global growth slowing, OPEC (led by Saudi Arabia) chose to defend market share rather than cut production. Brent fell from ~$115 in June 2014 to under $30 by early 2016.
OPEC and major non-OPEC producers (notably Russia) agreed to their first coordinated production cuts since 2008, forming the 'OPEC+' framework and helping stabilize prices after the 2014-16 crash.

event_name: US Withdraws from Iran Nuclear Deal
description: The US reimposed sanctions on Iranian oil exports, tightening global supply expectations and contributing to price gains through much of 2018.
Saudi Arabia and Russia failed to agree on COVID-19-related production cuts, triggering a price war with unexpected output increases just as demand was collapsing.

event_name: WTI Futures Turn Negative / COVID-19 Demand Collapse
description: Global lockdowns caused an unprecedented collapse in oil demand and a storage capacity crisis. US WTI futures briefly traded negative for the first time in history; Brent fell to around $20/bbl.
Russia's invasion triggered the largest sustained Brent rally since 2008 amid fears of Russian supply being cut off from global markets. Brent surged from ~$94 to briefly above $130/bbl.
